analogy: Think of "Globalisation and Social Change" through the chapter's own opening images: No discussion on social change in the twenty-first century can take place without some reference to globalisation. It is but natural that in this book on social change and development, the terms globalisation and liberalisation have already appeared in your earlier chapters. Recall the section... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 6.1 Are GlobAl InterconnectIons, 6.2 UnderstAndInG GlobAlIsAtIon, Globalisation and Social Change, Globalisation. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Globalisation and Social Change is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that no discussion on social change in the twenty-first century can take place without some reference to globalisation.

The text explains that it is but natural that in this book on social change and development, the terms globalisation and liberalisation have already appeared in your earlier chapters.

The text explains that recall the section on globalisation, liberalisation, and rural society in Chapter 4.

The text explains that go back and read the section on the Indian government’s policy of liberalisation and its impact on Indian industries in Chapter 5.

The text explains that it also came up when we discussed Vision Mumbai and the new visions for global cities in Chapter 3.

The text explains that other than your school books, you must have come across the term globalisation in newspapers, television programmes or even in everyday conversation.

The text explains that read any newspaper regularly for two weeks and note down how the term ‘globalisation’ is used.

The text explains that note down references to the term ‘globalisation’ and ‘global’ in different kinds of television programmes.

The text explains that you can focus on news and discussions on political or economic or cultural matters.

The text explains that but we still need to be clear about what exactly does the term means.

The text explains that in this chapter we seek to understand the meaning of globalisation, its different dimensions and their social consequences.

The text explains that however, this does not mean that there can be only one definition of globalisation and only one way of understanding it.

The text explains that indeed you will find that different subjects or academic disciplines may focus on different aspects of globalisation.

The text explains that economics may be dealing more with the economic dimensions such as capital flows.

The text explains that political science may focus on the changing role of governments.

The text explains that however, the very process of globalisation is so far-reaching that disciplines have to increasingly borrow from each other to understand both the causes and consequences of globalisation.

The text explains that what Sociology does is use the sociological imagination to make sense of the connections between the individual and society, the micro and the macro, the local and the global.

The text explains that and thereby affecting the way sociology has to study society.

The text explains that they are obviously also linked to public policies adopted by the government and its agreement with the World Trade Organisation (WTO).

The text explains that likewise macro policy changes have meant that instead of one television channel we have literally scores today.

The text explains that we will be discussing this in greater detail in the next chapter.

The text explains that the sociologist or social anthropologist cannot study society as though it was an isolated entity.

The text explains that we will see later in the chapter how traditional entertainers are affected.

The text explains that but as you saw its impact on different sections of society is very different.

The text explains that we also examine whether indeed globalisation has some distinctive features and if so what is it.

The text explains that we have read in our history textbooks about the famous Silk route, which centuries ago connected India to the great civilisations, which existed in China, Persia, Egypt and Rome.

The text explains that it is interesting to note that the greatest grammarian in Sanskrit namely Panini, who systematised and transformed Sanskrit grammar and phonetics around the fourth century BCE, was of Afghan origin. …The seventh-century Chinese scholar Yi Jing learned his Sanskrit in Java (in the city of Shri Vijaya) on his way from China to India.

The text explains that the scientific, cultural and economic history of the world would have been very limited indeed had we lived like well-frogs.

The text explains that you will recall from Chapter 1 that modern capitalism had a global dimension from its very inception.

The text explains that colonialism was part of the system that required new sources of capital, raw materials, energy, markets and a global network that sustained it.

The text explains that often globalisation today identifies large-scale movement of people or migration as a defining feature.

The text explains that so we need to ask ourselves whether the current process of change is radically different from anything we have seen in the past.

The text explains that we are also aware that western capitalism as it emerged in Europe was both built upon and maintained by global control over resources of other countries as in colonialism.

The text explains that the important question is, however, whether globalisation is just about global interconnections.

The text explains that or is it about some significant changes in the capitalist system of production and communication, organisation of labour and capital, technological innovations and cultural experiences, ways of governance and social movements?

The text explains that we seek to spell out some of the distinctive features of globalisation below.

The text explains that very broadly this process in India is termed as liberalisation.

The text explains that the term liberalisation refers to a range of policy decisions that the Indian state took since 1991 to open up the Indian economy to the world market.

The text explains that this marked a break with an earlier stated policy of the government to have a greater control over the economy.

The text explains that liberalisation of the economy meant the steady removal of the rules that regulated Indian trade and finance regulations.

The text explains that since July 1991, the Indian economy has witnessed a series of reforms in all major sectors of the economy (agriculture, industry, trade, foreign investment and technology, public sector, financial institutions, etc).

The text explains that the basic assumption was that greater integration into the global market would be beneficial to Indian economy.

The text explains that the process of liberalisation also involved the taking of loans from international institutions such as the International Monetary Fund (IMF).

The text explains that the government makes commitments to pursue certain kind of economic measures that involve a policy of structural adjustments.

The text explains that the electronic economy The ‘electronic economy’ is another factor that underpins economic globalisation.

The text explains that you have to draw from Chapter 4 and 5 to build up the story of globalisation and social change in India.

The text explains that in the recounting of this story, use your sociological imagination.

The text explains that choose any topic that is of interest to you and discuss how you think globalisation has affected it.

The text explains that you could choose cinema, work, marriage or any other topic.

The text explains that what are the distinctive features of a globalised economy?

The text explains that briefly discuss the impact of globalisation on culture.

The text explains that is it simply a market strategy adopted by multinational companies or is genuine cultural synthesis taking place?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 6: Globalisation and Social Change

prelims_traps: Do not treat 6.1 Are GlobAl InterconnectIons and 6.2 UnderstAndInG GlobAlIsAtIon as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Globalisation and Social Change; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Globalisation and Social Change through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 6.1 Are GlobAl InterconnectIons and 6.2 UnderstAndInG GlobAlIsAtIon to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Globalisation and Social Change, which statement is supported by the source text about Change?
options: What does it mean to the neighbourhood grocer if the retail sector is opened up to big transnational companies?

These are just few examples of the wide ranging and different kinds of changes that globalisation is bringing about.
